_comment: Example domain-specific config for a healthcare/biomedical corpus. Mirrors the CORD-19 scenario from the DataMorgana paper (Filice et al., 2025). Swap user categories for any other domain (legal, financial, technical support, etc.).

categories.name: factoid
categories.description: A question seeking a specific, concise piece of clinical or scientific information such as a drug name, dosage, date, or numerical finding.

categories.name: open_ended
categories.description: A question inviting a detailed explanation of a medical concept, treatment approach, or research finding.

categories.name: lay_language
categories.description: A question written in plain, everyday language that avoids medical jargon, as a member of the public would ask it.

categories.name: clinical_language
categories.description: A question written using precise medical or scientific terminology, as a healthcare professional or researcher would phrase it.

categories.name: similar_to_document
categories.description: A question phrased using the same technical terms and phrases appearing in the source document.

categories.name: distant_from_document
categories.description: A question phrased using terms completely different from those in the source document (e.g. common synonyms, alternative clinical names, lay descriptions).

categories.name: patient
categories.description: A regular patient who uses the system to get basic health information, understand symptoms, and learn about treatment options in accessible language.

categories.name: medical_doctor
categories.description: A medical doctor who needs to access advanced clinical information, treatment protocols, or drug interactions.

categories.name: clinical_researcher
categories.description: A clinical researcher who uses the system to access population health data, study methodologies, and research findings.

categories.name: public_health_authority
categories.description: A public health authority who uses the system to manage community health information and respond to health emergencies.
